pattern: The divisor for a substring is fixed by its own last digit, so tracking, for every possible small divisor 1-9, how many active start points currently sit at each remainder lets every substring ending at the current position be counted in O(1) amortized work instead of re-scanning it.
hints: A substring's last digit is also its divisor, and that divisor only ever takes one of 9 possible values -- so you can afford to track state separately for each of them.

For a fixed divisor d, as you extend the string one digit to the right, every previously-tracked start position's remainder updates the same way: new_remainder = (old_remainder * 10 + new_digit) mod d. Maintain a small remainder->count table per d and remap it each step, then add a fresh start at the current index.

After updating divisor d = current last digit's table for this position, the number of substrings ending here that are divisible by it is exactly the count sitting at remainder 0 in that table (this always includes the single-digit substring itself).

edge_inputs.description: statement example two, many divisibility hits
single zero digit, never counted as its own divisor
leading zeros inside substrings must still be read as a numeric value
all same non-zero digit, every substring divisible by itself
all zero digits, no substring ever counted
mixed digits with several zeros interleaved

stress.description: random digit string at scale, exercises the O(n) per-divisor remainder tracking
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    s = ''.join(rng.choice('0123456789') for _ in range(n))
    return [s]

complexity.time: O(n) (45 remainder slots tracked per position)
